text: memory of seeing them in use, Ronin understood Maul’s hand signals . Two enemies. Armed . “Eighty-five meters between us and that door. And they’ll have optics on us the moment we leave cover .” Ronin’s processors ramped up, tumbling through thousands of simulated approaches. Leaving cover was a necessity, but only opening fire on the guard bots immediately afterward had a high likelihood of success . The consequences of that action, however … He stepped away from the wall and unslung his rifle. Grasping it by the barrel, he held it out to Maul . “The hell are you doing?” the Sergeant asked . “Taking the most direct approach.” Ronin gestured to Dozer. “Give your weapon to Dodge .” “You must be in the throes of a critical error,” she replied, stone- faced . “Gunfire will draw attention. We don’t want that yet, not until we’re ready .” “So, the answer is to walk up to them and ask if we can go in?” Maul asked . “No. She’s going to walk up to them, dragging me along,” Ronin said . “And what will that accomplish, besides giving them some easy targets?” Dozer held her rifle across her chest, casually resting her left hand on the barrel. It was an incredibly human pose . “This is the Clinic. The place all the bots in Cheyenne come to for repairs. They see you dragging another bot with limited mobility up to the front doors, and their reaction will not be to shoot .” “I did some of the recon on this place,” she said, “and I’ve seen what some of them are packing. We’re asking to be scrapped, walking up there unarmed .” “From what the Sergeant said, it won’t matter what they’re packing if we can get you within arm’s reach .” She raised a brow, and one corner of her mouth scrunched. Skeptical, but intrigued? How much had these bots picked up from humans, living in the base for so long ? Maul leaned forward, taking another glance around the corner. He raised a fist before turning back to Ronin. “The other team is in position. Anything goes wrong, the two of you go prone, and we’ll fire from both angles. It’ll be noisy, but we’re not losing anyone just to take out these two bastards .” Dozer was silent and unmoving. Seconds ticked by on Ronin’s internal clock; to the east, pre-dawn light was touching the perpetually hazy sky. Bravo team had to be moving through the humans’ shacks now, guarding Lara as she roused her people. Warlord’s forces needed to be occupied before the humans amassed in the market. Otherwise, the people would be slaughtered . Finally, the female bot stirred. Without a word, she lifted the strap of her rifle over her head and handed the weapon to Dodge. Stepping forward, she glanced up at Ronin expectantly, spreading her open hands to either side . Ronin moved alongside her, turning to face the same direction, and slung an arm over her shoulders. When she wrapped hers around his waist, Lara’s face flashed up from his memory. He didn’t want to be so near to anyone but her. Despite the gravity of the situation, he wondered how she’d feel about the contact between himself and Dozer; would she be hurt or jealous? Why did his processors, against all logic, tell him this was wrong ? He eased his weight onto Dozer. She dipped slightly, for only a moment, before her actuators adjusted to compensate . “Heavy SOB. You retrofitted with a reinforced casing?” she asked . “ Apparently .” “Good. This goes wrong, I’m using you as a bullet shield .” He overrode the normal functioning of his actuators, locking his left knee and fully loosening the joints in his right. As he sagged forward, hanging his head to hide his face, Dozer clasped his wrist and tailored her posture to spread his weight . “Ready?” she asked . “ Yes .” She walked forward. Ronin’s legs dragged over the concrete. They rounded the corner, stepping onto the walkway. He kept his head down, optics on her boots . “What’s this?” the static-voiced gearhead demanded. Eighty-one meters to go . “He needs repairs,” Dozer answered. Seventy- three . “Haven’t seen you around before .” “Don’t have any record of your face on file,” Reg said . “We’re dustwalkers,” Ronin offered without raising his head, altering his voice modulation. “Just got into town. I took a spill in a ditch outside the wall .” “A ditch, or a canyon?” Reg asked. Fifty-seven meters . “His optics have been failing for years, and the last place he was repaired didn’t do a very good job,” Dozer said. Thirty-eight meters . “Brought some scrap in, and we were told this place provides the best repairs this side of the Dust,” Ronin explained. Variables fluttered through his processors, spiraling into countless scenarios of wildly fluctuating probability. They needed to get close, needed to seize the Clinic, needed to get to Lara and keep her safe . Dozer stopped, swaying slightly beneath Ronin’s weight. The wind whipped around the building, tugging at Ronin’s clothes. Thirty meters. Dozer’s actuators moved audibly within her casing . Electric crackled over Ronin’s cheek. He locked his arm to prevent himself from scratching . “They couldn’t be here if they weren’t admitted,” Reg reasoned . “Yes. You’re right,” static bot replied . Dozer resumed her walk. Her boots fell heavy on the concrete, which was run through with hastily-repaired cracks . Twenty-five meters. Fifteen. Seven . “How far have you been dragging him?” static bot asked . “Too damned far.” Three meters. “You mind helping me before I blow a motor ?” The gearheads came into view from their abdomens down; their pants were patched but clean, their boots weather-worn but sturdy. Their weapons — two ancient-looking assault rifles with cracked wooden stocks — swung through Ronin’s vision as they stepped forward. Slinging the rifles aside,
